full_text:
Insecurity continues to deteriorate in Tillabéry, Diffa, Tahoua, and Dosso regions 
Key Messages 
This report provides an update to the February to September 2026 Food Security Outlook and March 2026 Key 
Message Update. The analysis is based on information available as of April 22, 2026. 
Niger - Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026 
Crisis (IPC Phase 3) outcomes will likely persist in the regions 
of Tillabéry and Diffa, as well as in the northwestern part of 
Tahoua Region, from April through September 2026. Conflict 
continues to cause population displacement and disrupt 
livelihoods and markets. Prices exceeding the purchasing 
capacity of displaced populations and poor host households are 
resulting in limited market access, and households are reducing 
meals to one or two per day due to lack of food stocks. The 
number of households in Crisis (IPC Phase 3) is expected to 
increase from June to September 2026, and around 5 percent 
could deteriorate into Emergency (IPC Phase 4) under the 
combined effects of conflict and flooding. 
Stressed (IPC Phase 2) outcomes are expected in 
agricultural, agropastoral, and pastoral areas not affected 
by conflict from May through September 2026. Poor 
households’ purchasing power is only sufficient to cover food 
expenditures, while food stocks are being depleted and 
households increasingly depend on markets characterized by 
very high food prices. Small groups of poor households with 
more severe food deficits are expected to deteriorate into Crisis 
(IPC Phase 3) outcomes between June and September. Their 
limited purchasing power will only allow them to obtain very 
small quantities of food. 
The medium-term impacts of the conflict in the Middle East, 
driven by rising maritime freight costs and higher global oil 
prices, are expected to increase the prices of imported 
products and fertilizers. However, in the short term, market 
prices have not yet been disrupted by the conflict. Niger’s status 
as an oil-producing country and the diversity of supply sources 
for imported goods are helping to delay the transmission of 
ongoing disruptions to domestic markets for manufactured 
products. 
The security situation is deteriorating due to the increase 
and frequency of security incidents, leading to population 
displacement and disruptions to markets and livelihoods. The 
main insecurity hotspots Tillabéry, Diffa, northwestern Tahoua, 
and Dosso. 
FEWS NET is a United States Government-funded activity. The content of this report does not
necessarily reflect the view of the United States Government.
FEWS NET Niger
niger@fews.net
fews.net/west-africa/niger
Current anomalies in food security conditions as of April 2026 
Updates to key assumptions about atypical food security conditions through September 2026 
Most of the assumptions that underpinned FEWS NET’s analysis of the most likely scenario for the Niger Food 
Security Outlook from February to September 2026 remain valid; however, the following updates have been made 
to incorporate new evidence: 
Figure 1 
Number of security incidents and 
fatalities, January 2025 – March 2026 
Source: ACLED 
The security situation in Niger continues to deteriorate, 
with increasing attacks by armed terrorist groups in the 
regions of Tillabéry, Diffa, Tahoua, and Dosso. According to 
ACLED data, security incidents increased by 29 percent during 
the January-March 2026 period compared to the same period 
in 2025, with the largest increases recorded in Tahoua, Diffa, 
and Dosso (Figure 1). The analysis shows that terrorist attacks 
in 2026 caused an average of two fatalities per attack but were 
more deadly in the regions of Tahoua and Tillabéry, where the 
average reached three and four fatalities per attack, 
respectively. 
Terrorist attacks continue to trigger forced population 
displacement. According to UN High Commissioner for 
Refugees (UNHCR) data, as of March 31, 2026, the country 
had 1,032,114 forcibly displaced people, including 548,386 
internally displaced persons (IDPs), compared with 459,000 
during the same period in 2025, representing an increase of 19 percent. 
According to the final results of the 2025/26 agricultural season from the Ministry of Agriculture and 
Livestock, agricultural production declined by 10.7 percent compared with 2024/25, but remained 
similar to the five-year average. The largest declines were observed in the regions of Agadez, Diffa, Tahoua, 
and Tillabéry, where losses in cultivated area and production were recorded due to conflict and flooding. In 
pastoral areas, the fodder balance indicates a decline of more than 23 percent compared to last year due to 
rainfall deficits. This fodder deficit, combined with limited access to some grazing areas, is leading to an early 
onset of the lean season in pastoral zones beginning in April 2026. 
Markets are generally functioning normally overall, although major disruptions remain concentrated in 
markets located in conflict zones. Food supplies are considered sufficient due to local supplies from surplus-
producing farmers and traders, as well as government subsidized sales (82,000 metric tons of millet, sorghum, 
and maize). These subsidized sales, initiated in March 2026 by the Niger Food Products Office (OPVN), are 
expected to reach 820,000 households by the end of June 2026, or 1,435,000 people, in four phases. Demand is 
evolving steadily but remains lower than last year and below normal seasonal trends due to carryover stocks. 
Prices are generally lower than both last year’s levels and the five-year average. 
The continued influx of displaced people has led to an increase in labor supply, intensified competition, 
and below-average incomes. Seasonal income-generating activities for poor and displaced households – such 
as agricultural labor for the maintenance and harvesting of off-season crops, as well as land preparation for the 
upcoming rainy season – are being further constrained by the reduction in cultivated areas caused by 
insecurity. 
Food supply on markets will remain insufficient in conflict-affected areas. In addition to the agricultural 
production deficit, which limits local supply, insecurity continues to disrupt roads and trade flows used to supply 
markets. However, stocks from the 2025/26 harvest, combined with carryover stocks and the continued 
government ban on cereal exports, are expected to ensure food availability countrywide, particularly in areas 
unaffected by conflict. 
Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026
Famine Early Warning Systems Network 2
Projected acute food insecurity outcomes through September 2026 
Crisis (IPC Phase 3) outcomes are expected to persist in the regions of Tillabéry, Diffa, and northwestern 
Tahoua through September 2026. In these areas, poor households will deplete food stocks following very low 
agricultural production due to the combined effects of conflict and flooding. They will depend entirely on market 
purchases. Subsidized cereal sales generally contribute to stabilizing food prices in markets, but insecurity limits the 
geographic coverage of these operations. As a result, food price increases are expected to persist and worsen further 
due to the impacts of the conflict in the Middle East on transport costs and imported food products. These price 
increases are expected to reach their seasonal peak during this period, significantly restricting the purchasing power 
of poor and displaced households to very small quantities of food, insufficient to cover half of the required 2,100 
kilocalories per person per day. Between June and September 2026, Emergency (IPC Phase 4) outcomes are expected 
among a small proportion of poor displaced households (less than 5 percent) facing major livelihood disruptions due 
to theft and looting by armed terrorist groups and lacking income-earning opportunities in host areas. 
Agricultural, agropastoral, and pastoral areas unaffected by conflict are expected to face Stressed (IPC Phase 
2) outcomes from April through September 2026, with a small proportion of households in Crisis (IPC Phase 3) 
from June through September 2026. Poor households in these areas will deplete food stocks from own production 
and will rely on markets in a context of high prices and constrained purchasing power. However, substitution for 
cheaper food products will allow households to meet basic food needs. Food prices are expected to follow a 
sustained upward trend due to the effects of the lean season, progressively compounded by the impacts of the 
conflict in the Middle East, which are affecting fuel and imported food prices and transport costs. These disruptions 
will reduce the purchasing power of poor households in these areas from June through September 2026, limiting 
their expenditures primarily to food, while remaining insufficient to cover non-food needs. Small proportions of these 
poor households (around 5 percent) will lack sufficient food quantities, forcing households to reduce the number of 
meals or portion sizes, and causing gradual deterioration into Crisis (IPC Phase 3). 
Annex 1: Updates to key sources of evidence used in this analysis 
Many of the key sources of evidence utilized for FEWS NET’s February to September 2026 Food Security Outlook 
remain the same; however, new and additional sources of evidence are listed below. 
Poor households are expected to have low incomes due to the migration of populations from areas 
affected by agricultural deficits and increased competition in labor supply resulting from displacement 
from conflict areas. Income-earning opportunities from seasonal agricultural labor will be insufficient relative 
to labor supply because displaced persons are competing alongside poor host households for the same 
opportunities. 
Rainfall during the 2026/27 agricultural season is expected to be average to above average across 
Sahelian countries, according to the latest weather forecast updates. However, with these anticipated heavy 
rains, flood risks are also expected to remain high across the different hydrological basins. 
The conflict in the Middle East, with rising fuel and transport prices, is expected to increase fertilizer 
prices. This will likely reduce fertilizer use and decrease the cultivated area dedicated to fertilizer-intensive 
crops. These reductions in cultivated areas are expected to lower agricultural production, reduce demand for 
agricultural labor, and depress seasonal wages for poor households. 
The impacts of the ongoing conflict in the Middle East on global energy markets could lead food prices in 
Niger to rise above current projections, especially if maritime trade disruptions and higher fuel prices persist. 
In a context of heavy dependence on imports through already constrained (Cotonou) and costly (Lomé) trade 
corridors, any increase in transport costs is likely to be quickly passed on to food prices. When combined with 
the lean season, during which households become increasingly market dependent, these factors could further 
intensify inflationary pressures. 
Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026
Famine Early Warning Systems Network 3
Final results of the 2025/26 
agricultural season 
Ministry of 
Agriculture and 
Livestock 
Quantitative Total agricultural production compared to 
the previous year 
Rate of coverage of cereal requirements 
Food assistance Ministry of Trade/
government 
Quantitative Access to food assistance and its impact on 
meeting consumption needs 
Evidence Source Data 
format 
Food security element of analysis 
Annex 2: Seasonal calendar 
Recommended citation: FEWS NET. Niger Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026: Insecurity continues to 
deteriorate in Tillabéry, Diffa, Tahoua, and Dosso regions, 2026. 
Food Security Outlook Update 
This Food Security Outlook Update provides an analysis of current acute food insecurity conditions and any changes to FEWS NET's latest projection of acute food insecurity outcomes 
in the specified geography over the next six months. Learn more here. 
Evidence Source 
Data 
format Food security element of analysis 
Source: FEWS NET 
* FEWS NET classification is IPC-compatible with limited exceptions. IPC-compatible analysis follows key IPC protocols but does not necessarily reflect the consensus of national food security 
partners. As of IPC 3.0, the IPC no longer assesses the impact of food assistance on classification and thus no longer maps the (!). However, FEWS NET continues to produce food security 
maps inclusive of the (!) as well as maps compatible with IPC 3.0/3.1, which include the mapping of food security assistance bags. In rare cases, FEWS NET classification is IPC-aligned but 
not IPC-compatible. IPC-aligned analysis is evidence-based but some types of evidence required for IPC-compatibility were not available. 
Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026
Famine Early Warning Systems Network 4

related.title: Yemen: (Government-Controlled Areas) IPC Acute Food Insecurity Snapshot | March - December 2026 [EN/AR] (pdf)
related.summary: YEMEN (Government-Controlled Areas): IPC Acute Food Insecurity Snapshot  March - December 2026 Overview One in two people in Government of Yemen (GoY)-controlled areas of Yemen continue  experiencing high levels of acute food insecurity, with continued instability, localised  violence, security incidents, and a deteriorating macroeconomic crisis leaving millions  unable to meet basic food needs. Nearly 5 million people are experiencing IPC Phase 3 or above (Crisis or worse) conditions  between March and May 2026. Of these, 1.4 million people are experiencing IPC Phase 4  (Emergency), indicating large food consumption gaps and that people are undertaking  extreme coping strategies, such as selling their last female animal, selling their house or land,  and begging.
related.excerpt: YEMEN (Government-Controlled Areas): IPC Acute Food Insecurity Snapshot March - December 2026 Overview One in two people in Government of Yemen (GoY)-controlled areas of Yemen continue experiencing high levels of acute food insecurity, with continued instability, localised violence, security incidents, and a deteriorating macroeconomic crisis leaving millions unable to meet basic food needs. Nearly 5 million people are experiencing IPC Phase 3 or above (Crisis or worse) conditions between March and May 2026. Of these, 1.4 million people are experiencing IPC Phase 4 (Emergency), indicating large food consumption gaps and that people are undertaking extreme coping strategies, such as selling their last female animal, selling their house or land, and begging. related.title: Yemen: (Government-Controlled Areas) IPC Acute Food Insecurity Snapshot | March - December 2026 [EN/AR]
related.summary: YEMEN: One in two people in Government of Yemen-controlled areas face high levels of acute food insecurity amid economic crisis and insecurity One in two people in Government of Yemen (GoY)-controlled areas of Yemen continue experiencing high levels of acute food insecurity, with a deteriorating macroeconomic crisis, localised violence and civil unrest leaving millions unable to meet basic food needs. Nearly 5 million people are experiencing IPC Phase 3 or above (Crisis or worse) conditions between March and May 2026, with 1.4 million people experiencing IPC Phase 4 (Emergency). This is a marginal seasonal improvement in food availability and access compared to the previous period (September 2025–February 2026).
related.excerpt: YEMEN: One in two people in Government of Yemen-controlled areas face high levels of acute food insecurity amid economic crisis and insecurity One in two people in Government of Yemen (GoY)-controlled areas of Yemen continue experiencing high levels of acute food insecurity, with a deteriorating macroeconomic crisis, localised violence and civil unrest leaving millions unable to meet basic food needs. Nearly 5 million people are experiencing IPC Phase 3 or above (Crisis or worse) conditions between March and May 2026, with 1.4 million people experiencing IPC Phase 4 (Emergency). This is a marginal seasonal improvement in food availability and access compared to the previous period (September 2025–February 2026). However, these gains remain temporary and insufficient to offset persistent economic and structural pressures. The food security situation is expected to deteriorate during the J

related.title: The impacts of the Iranian crisis on Japan’s energy strategy
related.summary: Global Energy Agenda May 19, 2026 Print this page The impacts of the Iranian crisis on Japan’s energy strategy By Tatsuya Terazawa   Tatsuya Terazawa is the chairman and CEO of the Institute of Energy Economics, Japan. This essay is part of the  2026 Global Energy Agenda . The crisis triggered by the USｰIsraeli attack on Iran on February 28 has impacted the world in a serious manner.
related.excerpt: Global Energy Agenda May 19, 2026 Print this page The impacts of the Iranian crisis on Japan’s energy strategy By Tatsuya Terazawa Tatsuya Terazawa is the chairman and CEO of the Institute of Energy Economics, Japan. This essay is part of the 2026 Global Energy Agenda . The crisis triggered by the USｰIsraeli attack on Iran on February 28 has impacted the world in a serious manner. Japan is certainly not insulated from the crisis, especially in terms of the rising cost of energy. But despite its heavy dependence on imported energy, Japan has managed the crisis relatively well compared with most of its Asian neighbors. Five factors have helped Japan manage the crisis so far. The first is the high level of crude oil reserves. Japan had 254 days’ worth of reserves at the time of the attack. This level is much higher than those of other Asian countries, with the possible exception of China. T

related.title: Haiti - Food Security Outlook Update : Gas prices and insecurity worsen food access in Haiti, April - September 2026 (pdf)
related.summary: Gas prices and insecurity worsen food access in Haiti  Key Messages  This report provides an update to the February to September 2026  Food Security Outlook and March 2026 Key Message Update. The analysis is based on information available as of  April 15, 2026. Haiti - Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026  From April to September 2026, most of the country is  expected to remain in Crisis (IPC Phase 3).
related.excerpt: Gas prices and insecurity worsen food access in Haiti Key Messages This report provides an update to the February to September 2026 Food Security Outlook and March 2026 Key Message Update. The analysis is based on information available as of April 15, 2026. Haiti - Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026 From April to September 2026, most of the country is expected to remain in Crisis (IPC Phase 3). Certain communes in the Port-au-Prince Metropolitan Area (ZMPP), particularly Cité Soleil and Croix-des-Bouquets, are expected to face Emergency (IPC Phase 4) outcomes, with internally displaced persons (IDPs) especially affected. Pockets of households in Emergency (IPC Phase 4) are also expected in Artibonite, Centre, and some communes in the Nord-Ouest. These households, highly exposed to one or more forms of violence, deteriorating purchasing power, or rainfall irregularities,

related.title: US, Nigerian forces kill senior Islamic State leader
related.summary: US soldiers conduct training with Nigerian Army soldiers in a 2019 exercise. ( US Army )   The US military, “in  coordination  with the Armed Forces of Nigeria,” killed Abu Bilal al Minuki, a top Islamic State leader, and other unnamed commanders in an operation in northeastern Nigeria on May 16. Minuki was identified by US Africa Command (AFRICOM) as the “director of global operations” and the “ISIS [Islamic State] number two.”   “The command’s initial assessment is that multiple terrorists, to include Abu Bilal al Minuki, the director of global operations for ISIS, as well as other senior ISIS leaders, were killed during this operation,” AFRICOM  reported  in a press release.
related.excerpt: US soldiers conduct training with Nigerian Army soldiers in a 2019 exercise. ( US Army ) The US military, “in coordination with the Armed Forces of Nigeria,” killed Abu Bilal al Minuki, a top Islamic State leader, and other unnamed commanders in an operation in northeastern Nigeria on May 16. Minuki was identified by US Africa Command (AFRICOM) as the “director of global operations” and the “ISIS [Islamic State] number two.” “The command’s initial assessment is that multiple terrorists, to include Abu Bilal al Minuki, the director of global operations for ISIS, as well as other senior ISIS leaders, were killed during this operation,” AFRICOM reported in a press release. The Islamic State has not commented on reports of Minuki’s death. The operation that killed Minuki and the other Islamic State leaders “was made possible through the cooperation and coordination” of US and Nigerian forces

related.title: What Gulf states need in a US-Iran deal
related.summary: Bottom lines up front ABU DHABI—The talks that collapsed in Islamabad on April 12 were the first direct US-Iran conversation since 2015. After twenty-one hours, US Vice President JD Vance  announced  that there was no deal, that Iran had not accepted Washington’s terms, and that the naval blockade would be enforced. What he did not mention—and what apparently received too little attention during the marathon talks—was the six countries whose airports, energy infrastructure, and civilian neighborhoods had been struck by Iranian missiles and drones over the preceding forty days.
related.excerpt: Bottom lines up front ABU DHABI—The talks that collapsed in Islamabad on April 12 were the first direct US-Iran conversation since 2015. After twenty-one hours, US Vice President JD Vance announced that there was no deal, that Iran had not accepted Washington’s terms, and that the naval blockade would be enforced. What he did not mention—and what apparently received too little attention during the marathon talks—was the six countries whose airports, energy infrastructure, and civilian neighborhoods had been struck by Iranian missiles and drones over the preceding forty days. The Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) states were not parties to the Islamabad talks, and they do not appear to have been formally consulted at the time. While the GCC has sought to draw attention to their shared vulnerabilities, including at an April 28 summit in Jeddah , they have been too often overlooked in this cri

related.title: Do aid flotillas make a difference for Palestinians under Israeli blockade?
related.summary: Israeli minister’s mistreatment of abducted activists sparks global condemnation. Flotillas carrying humanitarian aid to Gaza began in 2008, two years after Israel imposed a blockade on the Strip. Two boats succeeded, despite Israeli attempts to stop them.
related.excerpt: Israeli minister’s mistreatment of abducted activists sparks global condemnation. Flotillas carrying humanitarian aid to Gaza began in 2008, two years after Israel imposed a blockade on the Strip. Two boats succeeded, despite Israeli attempts to stop them. Since then, Israeli forces have forced several flotillas to turn back, and detained hundreds of activists, sometimes violently and in international waters. The latest attack is drawing global condemnation for the abuse of the activists, notably by a senior Israeli minister . Various countries have summoned their Israeli envoys in protest. But will they go further and take action against Israel’s dehumanising treatment of activists and Palestinians? Presenter: Tom McRae Guests: Ahmed Abo Askar – Engineering student who lobbies for sponsorship of orphans in Gaza. Dan Perry – Former Chief Editor of the Associated Press in Europe, the Midd
